one_line_takeaway: EmbTracker embeds a per-client backdoor watermark purely by swapping trained trigger-token word-embedding vectors on the server before each round's distribution, giving black-box, individually traceable, near-100% VR watermarking for federated LLMs/VLMs with only 1-2% task-accuracy cost and much lower overhead than the prior black-box traceable baseline (TraMark), while surviving fine-tuning, pruning (<=30%), quantization, and noise attacks.
key_finding: Across BC/MC/QA/VQA tasks and both IID and Non-IID settings, EmbTracker achieves traceable VR close to 100% (e.g., 100.00% on SST-2 IID, 99.87% Non-IID; 98.06%/95.35% on Enron; down to 90.19%-99.99% range across most datasets) while WAFFLE's single shared watermark is marked as failing traceability (X) on every row despite similarly high raw VR, because it cannot distinguish clients; FedTracker (white-box) often falls below the gamma=90% traceability threshold on classification tasks (e.g., 87.27% on SST-2 IID, 86.72% on Enron IID) while EmbTracker exceeds 90% in essentially every row and is black-box. Against TraMark (the only other black-box traceable baseline, tested only on DBpedia and Yahoo per its label-count constraint), EmbTracker matches or exceeds VR with comparable ACC. Task-accuracy impact ('fidelity') versus vanilla FedAvg is typically within 1-2 percentage points, and in several cases EmbTracker's ACC exceeds FedAvg's (attributed to the disjoint watermark training set incidentally improving general ability). Time-efficiency analysis (Fig. 8) shows EmbTracker's overhead is only marginal versus FedAvg, in contrast to TraMark, whose per-client watermark retraining cost grows sharply with client count. Robustness results: fine-tuning attacks leave VR mostly above/near 90% (versus <10% leakage baseline); watermark survives pruning up to 30% of parameters (beyond which ACC itself collapses); quantization to INT8/FP16 keeps VR above 95% even as ACC drops; noise perturbation preserves watermark verifiability even when ACC degrades to pre-training levels; only a 5% poisoning ratio is needed to exceed 95% VR on NQ (Table V: VR rises from 13.76% at ratio 0.01 to 96.43% at 0.05, 99.19% at 0.5); as few as 1-10 training epochs already exceed 95% VR (Table VI). An adaptive 'overwriting' attack, where a malicious client tries to implant a competing watermark into the same embedding slot, leaves the original watermark's VR still high (98.43% after attack vs 100.00% before) while the new watermark also verifies (99.67%) -- the paper proposes a timestamped-signature/CA-registration extension to resolve the resulting ownership ambiguity, but does not implement or evaluate it experimentally.
core_contribution: EmbTracker, a server-side, black-box, client-traceable backdoor watermarking framework for Federated Language Models (FedLMs). Unlike prior FL watermarking that either shares one watermark across all clients (only group-level ownership proof) or requires white-box access / client-side cooperation, EmbTracker embeds a unique identity-specific watermark into the word-embedding vector of a designated trigger token for each client, entirely on the server side, so a leaked model copy can be black-box-attributed to the specific client that leaked it. The method trains a single 'universal' watermark embedding once (via embedding-poisoning-style training that updates only the trigger token's embedding, freezing all other parameters), then for each client swaps in that trained embedding at the position of a client-specific trigger token (derived by hashing a digitally-signed client identity message to a vocabulary index), avoiding any per-client retraining. The word-embedding layer is never touched by client-side PEFT (LoRA/prefix-tuning) updates, so the watermark survives federated aggregation rounds. The paper also proposes extending this design to vision-language models (via the LLM's word-embedding layer) and demonstrates it on BERT, Llama-2-7B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.2-3B, Vicuna-7B, and Mistral-7B.
threat_model: Federated learning with one server (trusted defender) and K clients, some of whom are malicious. Malicious clients follow the FL protocol faithfully (do not deviate from training) but may illegally redistribute/leak the model instance they receive for personal profit. Attackers are assumed unaware of the watermarking process and do not collude with each other. The defender (server) has full control over watermark injection, access to its own auxiliary dataset for constructing the watermark training set, and standard FL capabilities (distributes global model, receives client updates each round) -- no assumption of white-box access to a leaked/suspicious model at verification time (verification is black-box, via API queries). Adversarial capabilities tested empirically include fine-tuning, parameter pruning (up to 60%), quantization (INT8/FP16/FP32), additive noise perturbation, and an adaptive 'overwriting' attack where a malicious client, aware of the mechanism, tries to implant its own watermark into the same embedding to obfuscate the original.
limitations: The paper's own text (not inferred): (1) The overwriting/adaptive-attack mitigation (timestamping identity messages and CA-registered order of trigger claims) is proposed only conceptually in Section V-K -- it is not implemented or empirically evaluated, so multi-watermark ambiguity is not actually resolved by demonstrated results. (2) TraMark comparisons are restricted to DBpedia and Yahoo because TraMark's algorithm requires the number of classification labels to be >= number of clients, so the head-to-head evidence against the only other black-box traceable baseline is narrower than the rest of the evaluation. (3) The threat model explicitly assumes non-colluding, protocol-following malicious clients unaware of the watermarking process; it does not evaluate collusion between multiple malicious clients or a client that deviates from the FL training protocol itself. (4) Server trust is a core assumption (the server is 'generally considered more trustworthy' and is the sole defender/injector); a malicious or compromised server is out of scope. (5) At high pruning rates (>30%) the paper notes the model itself becomes unusable, so robustness is only meaningfully claimed up to that threshold, not beyond it. (6) Quantization robustness is reported even though ACC 'has decreased significantly' at some quantization levels, meaning high VR is retained partly because the underlying model utility is already degraded, which the paper does not deeply analyze as a caveat.

datasets_used: WebQA (used only as an auxiliary fine-tuning-attack dataset in Fig. 9)
quantitative_results: SST-2 IID: EmbTracker ACC 96.14 / VR 100.00 vs FedAvg ACC 96.37 vs WAFFLE ACC 90.25/VR 100.00(fails traceability) vs FedTracker ACC 89.22/VR 87.27(fails)
Yahoo IID: EmbTracker ACC 72.10 / VR 99.48 vs TraMark ACC 71.96 / VR 98.46
DBpedia IID: EmbTracker ACC 98.40 / VR 100.00 vs TraMark ACC 99.10 / VR 90.19
FreebaseQA IID: EmbTracker ACC 52.74 / VR 99.90 vs FedAvg ACC 54.13 vs WAFFLE ACC 49.67/VR 93.33(fails) vs FedTracker ACC 51.33/VR 95.78 vs TraMark ACC 51.83/VR 90.05
OK-VQA IID: EmbTracker ACC 46.30 / VR 96.06 vs FedAvg ACC 49.74
OCR-VQA IID: EmbTracker ACC 74.29 / VR 99.20 vs FedAvg ACC 75.86
Poison ratio ablation on NQ (Table V): VR = 13.76% at ratio 0.01, 80.81% at 0.02, 96.43% at 0.05, 98.57% at 0.1, 98.90% at 0.2, 99.19% at 0.5
Watermark training epochs ablation (Table VI): VR = 97.76% (1 epoch), 97.19% (2), 97.23% (3), 97.76% (4), 98.57% (5), 98.90% (10)
PEFT applicability (Table III), FreebaseQA: LoRA ACC 52.74/VR 99.90 vs Prefix Tuning ACC 51.67/VR 99.62 (w/o WM baseline 54.13 and 51.60 respectively)
FL-algorithm applicability (Table IV), NQ: FedAvg ACC 74.67/VR 98.57, FedAvgM ACC 72.98/VR 95.05, FedProx ACC 73.00/VR 99.00, SCAFFOLD ACC 74.67/VR 98.43
Overwriting attack (Table VII): VR before attack 100.00; after attack, original watermark VR 98.43, new (attacker) watermark VR 99.67
Single trigger embedding parameter count for Llama-2-7B: 4096 parameters, ~6x10^-7 of total model parameters
Pruning robustness: high VR and negligible ACC drop maintained up to 30% parameter pruning; beyond that ACC drops below pre-training level
Quantization robustness: VR remains above 95% under FP16/INT8/FP32 despite ACC dropping significantly at aggressive settings
baselines_compared: WAFFLE (server-side universal watermark, no client traceability)
TraMark (server-side black-box traceable watermark, classification-only, requires #labels >= #clients)
